name: Augustus
hint: Took the name of his uncle; some say his wife poisoned him with figs.
description: Augustus was the first Roman emperor, rising to power after defeating Mark Antony and becoming the adopted heir of Julius Caesar. His reign of over 40 years established the imperial system and ushered in the Pax Romana.

name: Tiberius
hint: A reluctant ruler who isolated himself on an island.
description: Tiberius was the second Roman emperor, a skilled military commander who withdrew to the island of Capri for the last decade of his reign. He ruled remotely while rumors of scandalous behavior on the island spread through Rome.

name: Caligula
hint: Named for something he wore as a toddler, he believed himself to be a god.
description: Caligula was the third Roman emperor, known for his increasingly erratic and cruel behavior after a severe illness in 37 AD. He was assassinated by the Praetorian Guard after less than four years on the throne.

name: Claudius
hint: His stammer made others underestimate him, also conquered an island.
description: Claudius was the fourth Roman emperor, overlooked for decades due to physical disabilities and a stammer before being proclaimed emperor by the Praetorian Guard after Caligula's assassination. He proved a capable administrator and oversaw the Roman conquest of Britain.

name: Nero
hint: A teenage artist whose reign went up in flames.
description: Nero was the last Julio-Claudian emperor, coming to power at sixteen through his mother Agrippina's machinations. He is remembered for performing publicly as an artist, the Great Fire of Rome in 64 AD, and his eventual suicide as rebellion closed in.

name: Galba
hint: Stingy to a fatal degree, he lost his supporters almost as fast as he gained them.
description: Galba was an elderly senator and governor of Hispania Tarraconensis who seized power after Nero's fall. His refusal to pay promised bonuses to the Praetorian Guard quickly turned his supporters against him, and he was murdered after just seven months.

name: Otho
hint: Decided to kill himself to avoid further bloodshed after losing a civil war.
description: Otho had been exiled to Lusitania after a conflict with Nero over Poppaea Sabina. He overthrew Galba but was defeated by Vitellius's forces at the Battle of Bedriacum and took his own life after a reign of just three months.

name: Vitellius
hint: A large, hungry man whose appetite dwarfed his impact.
description: Vitellius was proclaimed emperor by his Rhine legions and defeated Otho to claim the throne. He was known for extravagant feasting and was dragged through the streets of Rome, tortured, and killed when Vespasian's forces took the capital.

name: Vespasian
hint: "A stingy military man who proclaimed, ""Money does not stink!"""
description: Vespasian was a career military officer who founded the Flavian dynasty after emerging victorious from the civil war of 69 AD. He stabilized the empire's finances and began construction of the Colosseum in Rome.

name: Titus
hint: Natural disasters defined his brief time in charge, but people loved him anyway.
description: Titus completed the siege of Jerusalem and destroyed the Second Temple in 70 AD before becoming emperor. His short reign was marked by the eruption of Vesuvius in 79 AD that buried Pompeii and Herculaneum.

name: Domitian
hint: A true authoritarian, his paranoia led to his assassination despite successful monetary reforms.
description: Domitian was the younger son of Vespasian who became emperor after his brother Titus's early death. An effective but increasingly paranoid administrator, he was assassinated in a palace conspiracy organized by members of his own court.

name: Nerva
hint: His greatest achievement was choosing his successor.
description: Nerva was an elderly senator chosen as emperor after Domitian's assassination, lacking military support or a strong power base. He stabilized his position by adopting the popular general Trajan as his heir, establishing the tradition of adoptive succession.

name: Trajan
hint: He hailed from the provinces and brought Rome to its territorial peak.
description: Trajan was the first Roman emperor born outside Italy, hailing from Italica in Hispania. He expanded the empire to its greatest territorial extent through conquests in Dacia and Mesopotamia, and is remembered as one of the 'Five Good Emperors.'

name: Hadrian
hint: He never recovered from the drowning of a lover.
description: Hadrian spent much of his reign traveling the empire and commissioned the famous wall in northern Britain that bears his name. He was devastated by the drowning of his beloved companion Antinous in the Nile in 130 AD.

name: Antoninus Pius
hint: Was given his cognomen for his piety to his disliked predecessor.
description: Antoninus Pius earned his cognomen by persuading the Senate to deify his predecessor Hadrian. His 23-year reign was one of the most peaceful in Roman history, notable for its stability and lack of major military conflict.

name: Marcus Aurelius
hint: A philosopher on a frozen frontier.
description: Marcus Aurelius was a Stoic philosopher-emperor who wrote his famous Meditations in Greek during campaigns along the Danube. He broke the tradition of adoptive succession by passing power to his biological son Commodus, a decision widely criticized by historians.

name: Lucius Verus
hint: Overshadowed by a stoic.
description: Lucius Verus served as co-emperor alongside Marcus Aurelius, the first time imperial power was formally shared between equals. He oversaw the Parthian War but his returning armies brought the devastating Antonine Plague back to the empire.

name: Commodus
hint: Renamed everything from months to the city after himself.
description: Commodus was the son of Marcus Aurelius and the last of the Nerva-Antonine dynasty, ending the era of adoptive succession. He became obsessed with gladiatorial combat, personally fought in the arena, and renamed Rome and the months of the year after himself before being assassinated.

name: Pertinax
hint: From classroom to throne in a lifetime, then back to nothing in under ninety days.
description: Pertinax was a former schoolteacher who rose through the military to become emperor after Commodus's assassination. He lasted only 87 days before the Praetorian Guard, unhappy with his fiscal discipline, murdered him.

name: Didius Julianus
hint: Quite literally bought the empire.
description: Didius Julianus was a wealthy senator who purchased the throne at an auction held by the Praetorian Guard after Pertinax's murder. His legitimacy was never accepted by the provincial armies, and he was abandoned and killed within weeks.

name: Septimius Severus
hint: Began a dynasty whose only competent Emperor was himself.
description: Septimius Severus was born in Leptis Magna in North Africa and won a civil war to establish the Severan dynasty. He died at York in Britain in 211 AD, reportedly advising his sons to enrich the soldiers and disregard everyone else.

name: Geta
hint: Murdered by his brother, in the hands of their Mother.
description: Geta briefly co-ruled with his brother Caracalla, who had him murdered in their mother Julia Domna's arms during a reconciliation meeting. Caracalla then subjected Geta's memory to damnatio memoriae, erasing his name and image from public records.

name: Caracalla
hint: Murdered his brother, in the hands of their Mother.
description: Caracalla extended Roman citizenship to nearly all free inhabitants of the empire through the Constitutio Antoniniana in 212 AD. He was obsessed with Alexander the Great and built the enormous Baths of Caracalla in Rome, whose ruins still partially stand.

name: Macrinus
hint: Lost his throne to a teenage priest backed by scheming women.
description: Macrinus was the first emperor who had never been a senator, having served as Praetorian prefect under Caracalla. He was suspected of orchestrating Caracalla's assassination and was overthrown after barely a year by the Severan women's candidate, Elagabalus.

name: Elagabalus
hint: A sexually debauched foreign prince who believed himself to be a god.
description: Elagabalus was a teenage Syrian priest of the sun god El-Gabal, installed as emperor through the political machinations of his grandmother Julia Maesa. His religious practices and personal behavior scandalized Rome, and he was murdered by the Praetorian Guard at about eighteen.

name: Severus Alexander
hint: His mother ran everything, and his soldiers despised him for preferring diplomacy to fighting.
description: Severus Alexander became emperor as a teenager, with his mother Julia Mamaea and grandmother Julia Maesa dominating the government. His attempt to negotiate with Germanic tribes rather than fight them led to a military mutiny that cost him his life in 235 AD.

name: Maximinus Thrax
hint: A giant who was killed by his own men at a siege.
description: Maximinus Thrax was reputedly a Thracian peasant of enormous physical size who rose through the army to become emperor. He was the first emperor never to set foot in Rome and was killed by his own soldiers during the siege of Aquileia.

name: Gordian I
hint: Pushed into the job in his late seventies, he lasted three weeks before tragic news ended him.
description: Gordian I was an elderly proconsul in his late seventies reluctantly proclaimed emperor during a revolt in Africa. When his son Gordian II was killed in battle, he committed suicide after a reign of only about three weeks.

name: Gordian II
hint: He did the actual fighting while his ancient father held the title, and his death ended both.
description: Gordian II was co-emperor with his elderly father Gordian I in Africa, leading their forces in the field. He was killed in battle against the governor of Numidia, and his death triggered his father's immediate suicide.

name: Pupienus
hint: A man with an unfortunate name, whose reign lasted 99 days.
description: Pupienus was an experienced senator and military man chosen jointly with Balbinus by the Senate to oppose Maximinus Thrax. The two co-emperors despised each other and were both murdered by the Praetorian Guard after only 99 days.

name: Balbinus
hint: An orator forced to share power with a soldier he despised, with predictable results.
description: Balbinus was a nobleman and orator chosen jointly with Pupienus by the Senate as co-emperor. The two could not cooperate, and both were murdered by the Praetorian Guard after only 99 days in power.

name: Gordian III
hint: A boy ruler whose father-in-law ran everything until both died far from home.
description: Gordian III became emperor at about thirteen years old, with his father-in-law Timesitheus serving as chief minister and Praetorian prefect. He died under mysterious circumstances during a campaign against Persia, possibly at the instigation of Philip the Arab.

name: Philip I
hint: He threw the city its thousandth birthday party.
description: Philip the Arab was born in the province of Arabia and rose to power after the mysterious death of Gordian III. He presided over the celebration of Rome's one thousandth anniversary in 248 AD before being killed in battle against the usurper Decius.

name: Decius
hint: Sent to stop a rebellion, he joined it instead, then made history by dying on a foreign battlefield.
description: Decius was a senator sent to suppress a revolt but was proclaimed emperor by the very troops he was meant to discipline. He initiated the first empire-wide persecution of Christians and became the first Roman emperor to die in battle against a foreign enemy, falling to the Goths at the Battle of Abritus.

name: Trebonianus Gallus
hint: He inherited a disaster on the battlefield and made a peace deal his own men never forgave.
description: Trebonianus Gallus took power on the battlefield after both Decius and Herennius Etruscus died fighting the Goths at Abritus. He made an unpopular peace treaty with the Goths and was killed by his own soldiers when the general Aemilianus marched against him.

name: Hostilian
hint: A forgotten son who vanished from the record within weeks, probably taken by plague.
description: Hostilian was the son of Decius who was briefly recognized as co-emperor under Trebonianus Gallus. He died within weeks, most likely from plague, making his one of the least documented reigns in Roman history.

name: Aemilianus
hint: One lucky victory made him ruler; two months later his luck ran out.
description: Aemilianus was a general on the Danube frontier whose victory against the Goths led his troops to proclaim him emperor. His reign lasted only about two months before his own soldiers killed him when Valerian approached with a larger army.

name: Valerian
hint: Legend says he was skinned after capture.
description: Valerian was an older senator who took power during a chaotic period and became the only Roman emperor captured alive by a foreign enemy. He was taken prisoner by the Sasanian king Shapur I after the Battle of Edessa and never returned.

name: Gallienus
hint: Fifteen years of holding together something that was falling apart on every side.
description: Gallienus ruled for fifteen years during one of the most chaotic periods in Roman history, facing constant usurpers while large portions of the empire broke away. Despite these crises, he reformed the cavalry and patronized intellectual and artistic life.

name: Claudius Gothicus
hint: A plague took him just as he'd earned a famous victory title.
description: Claudius II earned the title 'Gothicus' by defeating a large Gothic invasion at the Battle of Naissus in 268 AD. His reign was cut short by plague after less than two years, and he was later claimed as an ancestor by Constantine I.

name: Quintillus
hint: His brother's fame couldn't save him and was overthrown by a sun worshipper.
description: Quintillus was the brother of Claudius II who briefly claimed the throne after his brother's death from plague. He was quickly challenged by the general Aurelian and took his own life after a reign of only a few weeks.

name: Aurelian
hint: The restorer of the world.
description: Aurelian reunified the Roman Empire by reconquering the breakaway Palmyrene Empire in the east and the Gallic Empire in the west. He built the Aurelian Walls around Rome and earned the title Restitutor Orbis (Restorer of the World).

name: Tacitus
hint: Shares his name with a famous writer, but had no one write much about him.
description: Tacitus was an elderly senator reportedly chosen by the Senate as emperor, a rare throwback to older traditions of senatorial authority. He shares his name with the famous historian Tacitus but was unrelated, and he died after about six months under unclear circumstances.

name: Florianus
hint: The brother of a man who shares his name with a famous writer.
description: Florianus claimed the throne as the half-brother of Tacitus without waiting for Senate approval. He was abandoned by his own troops and killed after less than three months when the general Probus gathered a larger army in the east.

name: Probus
hint: His soldiers killed him for making them do farmwork between battles.
description: Probus was a career soldier from the Balkans who spent most of his reign campaigning against Germanic and other invasions along the frontiers. He was killed by his own soldiers, reportedly because he forced them to perform agricultural labor during peacetime.

name: Carus
hint: Allegedly struck down by lightning.
description: Carus was a Praetorian prefect who seized power and launched a successful invasion of the Sasanian Empire, capturing the capital Ctesiphon. He died mysteriously in his tent, with official accounts claiming he was struck by lightning.

name: Carinus
hint: His appetites reportedly got him killed mid-battle by one of his own wronged officers.
description: Carinus was left to govern the western provinces while his father Carus campaigned in Persia. He developed a terrible reputation and was killed at the Battle of the Margus, possibly by one of his own officers whose wife he had seduced.

name: Numerian
hint: Found dead in a sealed carriage while the army marched, with someone trying to cover it up.
description: Numerian accompanied his father Carus on the Persian campaign and was left in command of the eastern army after his father's sudden death. He was found dead in his litter while the army marched home, his death concealed for days by his father-in-law, the prefect Arrius Aper.

name: Diocletian
hint: Chose cabbages over power.
description: Diocletian rose from humble origins to create the Tetrarchy, a system of four co-emperors governing different parts of the empire. He is one of the very few Roman emperors to voluntarily abdicate, retiring to his palace at Split in Dalmatia.

name: Maximian
hint: He couldn't accept retirement and kept coming back until it killed him.
description: Maximian was appointed co-emperor by Diocletian and given charge of the western half of the empire. After being forced to abdicate alongside Diocletian, he repeatedly attempted to reclaim power and was eventually killed after his final failed plot.

name: Galerius
hint: A lifelong persecutor who reversed course on his deathbed.
description: Galerius served as junior co-emperor in the east and was one of the most aggressive promoters of the persecution of Christians. On his deathbed he issued a surprising edict of toleration, and he built a grand arch and palace complex in Thessaloniki.

name: Constantius I
hint: Died at a northern outpost where his famous son was waiting.
description: Constantius I (Chlorus) served as junior co-emperor governing Britain and Gaul, known for his relatively mild treatment of Christians during the Great Persecution. He died at York in 306 AD, where his son Constantine was immediately proclaimed emperor by the troops.

name: Severus II
hint: His own army melted away when he tried to use it.
description: Severus II was appointed as junior co-emperor in the west but lacked a strong power base. When he marched against the usurper Maxentius in Rome, his own troops deserted him, and he was captured and eventually killed.

name: Maxentius
hint: He drowned just outside the city at a bridge that became legendary.
description: Maxentius was the son of Maximian who seized power in Rome after being excluded from the Tetrarchic succession. He built a massive basilica in the Roman Forum but was defeated and drowned at the Battle of the Milvian Bridge in 312 AD.

name: Licinius
hint: He co-signed a famous proclamation of freedom, then went to war with his co-signer.
description: Licinius served as co-emperor in the east and co-issued the Edict of Milan with Constantine in 313 AD, granting religious freedom throughout the empire. Their alliance broke down into civil war, and Licinius was defeated and executed.

name: Maximinus II Daza
hint: The last holdout against a rising faith, he died running from a battlefield.
description: Maximinus II Daza was a nephew of Galerius who was elevated to junior co-emperor in the east. He was the last Roman emperor to actively persecute Christians and died in 313 AD while fleeing after his defeat by Licinius.

name: Constantine I
hint: By this sign, he conquered.
description: Constantine I was proclaimed emperor by his father's troops at York and spent years in civil wars to become sole ruler. He founded Constantinople, convened the Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, and transformed the empire by embracing Christianity.

name: Constantine II
hint: Had the name of a great, but was beaten by his younger brother.
description: Constantine II was the eldest son of Constantine I and inherited the western provinces. He quarreled with his younger brother Constans over territory and was killed in an ambush near Aquileia after just three years of rule.

name: Constans I
hint: Undone by a barbarian officer, the sources accuse him of homosexuality.
description: Constans I was the youngest son of Constantine I, gaining control of the western empire after his brother Constantine II's death. He was overthrown by the usurper Magnentius in 350 AD and killed while fleeing.

name: Constantius II
hint: Purged his family, then outlasted his brothers.
description: Constantius II was the middle son of Constantine I who eventually became sole emperor. He spent much of his reign fighting the Sasanian Persians and promoted Arianism, a theological position on the nature of Christ that was ultimately rejected by church councils.

name: Magnentius
hint: The civil war he lost bled the army so badly it never fully recovered.
description: Magnentius was a military officer of Germanic descent who overthrew the emperor Constans in 350 AD. He lost a devastating civil war to Constantius II, with the Battle of Mursa Major producing casualties so severe it permanently weakened the Roman military.

name: Vetranio
hint: He gave the job back voluntarily and got a quiet retirement as a reward.
description: Vetranio was an elderly general proclaimed emperor by his Danubian troops during the civil unrest following Constans's death. He voluntarily surrendered power to Constantius II after only a few months and was allowed to retire peacefully.

name: Julian
hint: A bookworm turnt emperor, he tried to reverse the tide of history.
description: Julian was raised in scholarly seclusion and unexpectedly proved a brilliant military commander in Gaul. He famously rejected Christianity and attempted to restore traditional pagan worship, dying from a spear wound during a campaign against the Sasanian Empire in 363 AD.

name: Jovian
hint: Picked in a panic after a death on campaign, he died possibly to paint.
description: Jovian was a military officer hastily chosen as emperor after Julian's death during the Persian campaign. He negotiated an unfavorable peace treaty ceding territory to the Sasanians and died mysteriously after ruling for only about eight months.

name: Valentinian I
hint: An angry man who was the last Emperor to campaign across the Rhine.
description: Valentinian I was a career soldier from Pannonia selected as emperor by a council of military officers. Known for his violent temper, he kept two pet bears near his bedchamber and died of a stroke in 375 AD, reportedly while shouting at a Quadi delegation.

name: Valens
hint: Mishandled immigrants and died from unaccounted infantry.
description: Valens was given the eastern half of the empire by his brother Valentinian I and spent much of his reign dealing with Gothic refugees who crossed the Danube. He died at the Battle of Adrianople in 378 AD, a defeat often cited as a pivotal moment in the empire's decline.

name: Procopius
hint: He came out of hiding to grab the capital while the real ruler was away, and it didn't last.
description: Procopius was a relative of the emperor Julian who emerged from hiding to seize Constantinople while Valens was away. He briefly gained support by presenting himself as a dynastic heir but was betrayed by his own supporters and executed.

name: Gratian
hint: "Elevated a ""great"", and died to another ""great""."
description: Gratian became western emperor as a teenager and offended the traditional Roman senatorial class by removing the Altar of Victory from the Senate house. He elevated Theodosius to replace Valens after the disastrous battle of Adrianople. He was abandoned by his army in favour of the usurper Magnus Maximus from Britain and was murdered in 383 AD.

name: Magnus Maximus
hint: The great, the greatest.
description: Magnus Maximus was a military commander in Britain who was proclaimed emperor by his troops and crossed to Gaul to seize power. He became a legendary figure in Welsh and British tradition and was eventually defeated and executed by Theodosius I.

name: Valentinian II
hint: A lifelong puppet found hanging, starting a civil war.
description: Valentinian II became western emperor as a small child and spent his entire reign controlled by powerful generals and advisors. He was found dead by hanging, officially ruled a suicide, though murder by the Frankish general Arbogast was widely suspected.

name: Eugenius
hint: A rhetoric teacher used as a front man for the last real push to bring back the old gods.
description: Eugenius was a rhetoric teacher elevated to the western throne as a puppet by the Frankish general Arbogast. He represented the last serious attempt to restore pagan influence in the empire and was defeated by Theodosius I at the Battle of the Frigidus in 394 AD.

name: Theodosius I
hint: A Spaniard who split the empire and elevated the Christians.
description: Theodosius I was a Spaniard recalled to service after the disaster at Adrianople and made emperor of the east. He made Christianity the official state religion, outlawed pagan worship, and was the last emperor to rule both halves of the Roman Empire.

name: Arcadius
hint: His ministers and his wife ran everything while he wore the crown.
description: Arcadius became eastern Roman emperor at about seventeen and was dominated throughout his reign by powerful ministers like Rufinus and Eutropius, as well as his wife Eudoxia. Despite his weak personal rule, the eastern empire remained relatively stable.

name: Honorius
hint: Allegedly devastated when he heard news his chicken had perished, relieved when it was Rome instead.
description: Honorius ruled the western Roman Empire for nearly thirty years while it disintegrated around him, losing Britain and watching as Rome was sacked by the Visigoths in 410 AD. He was reportedly more upset about the death of a favorite chicken named Roma than the fall of the city.

name: Constantine III
hint: Rose to power in Britain, and some say he was the grandfather of King Arthur.
description: Constantine III was a common soldier in Britain proclaimed emperor, possibly because of his auspicious name echoing Constantine I. He crossed to Gaul and briefly controlled much of the western provinces before being defeated, captured, and executed.

name: Theodosius II
hint: Crowned at seven, he left behind legal and physical walls that lasted for centuries.
description: Theodosius II became eastern emperor at age seven and reigned for over 42 years, one of the longest reigns in Roman history. He is best known for the Codex Theodosianus and the massive Theodosian Walls that protected Constantinople for centuries.

name: Constantius III
hint: The real power who finally got the title to match, only to die months later.
description: Constantius III was a powerful general who effectively ran the western empire and married Galla Placidia, the sister of Honorius, to legitimize his position. He was briefly recognized as co-emperor but died of illness after only about seven months.

name: Joannes
hint: A bureaucrat who sat on a throne that wasn't his for about a year before a very public end.
description: Joannes was a senior civil servant placed on the western throne by the general Castinus after the death of Honorius. He was overthrown by an eastern military expedition after about a year, captured, publicly mutilated, and executed.

name: Valentinian III
hint: He stabbed his best defender in a jealous rage, then the dead man's friends returned the favor.
description: Valentinian III was placed on the western throne as a child by the eastern court and reigned for nearly thirty years. He personally murdered his general Aetius in a fit of jealousy and was himself assassinated shortly after by Aetius's supporters.

name: Marcian
hint: He called a famous council and told a feared warlord he wouldn't be paying anymore.
description: Marcian was a retired military officer who became eastern emperor by marrying Pulcheria, the sister of Theodosius II. He refused to pay tribute to Attila the Hun and convened the Council of Chalcedon in 451 AD, which defined orthodox Christological doctrine.

name: Petronius Maximus
hint: He may have engineered the vacancy he filled, then sea raiders showed up two months later.
description: Petronius Maximus was a wealthy senator who schemed to take the western throne after Valentinian III's assassination, which he may have helped arrange. His reign lasted barely two months before the Vandals arrived to sack Rome and he was killed trying to flee.

name: Avitus
hint: His barbarian allies got him the job, but his accent and connections cost him everything.
description: Avitus was a Gallic aristocrat and diplomat who was proclaimed western emperor with Visigothic support. He was forced to abdicate after little more than a year when Italian factions turned against his Gallic connections.

name: Majorian
hint: Showed potential but was cut short by burning boats and a betrayal.
description: Majorian is considered by many historians to be the last competent western Roman emperor, personally leading campaigns to reassert control over Gaul and Spain. His plan to invade Vandal Africa was sabotaged when his fleet was destroyed, and he was deposed and executed by his generalissimo Ricimer.

name: Libius Severus
hint: A puppet controlled by the barbarian magister militum.
description: Libius Severus was a puppet emperor installed by the barbarian general Ricimer, who held the real power in the western empire. He was never recognized by the eastern court and died after about four years, possibly poisoned.

name: Anthemius
hint: Sent from the east to fix the west, his big naval gamble failed spectacularly.
description: Anthemius was an eastern aristocrat and former general sent west by the eastern court to rule. His joint naval expedition with the east against the Vandals ended in a catastrophic defeat, and he was killed during a conflict with Ricimer in Rome.

name: Olybrius
hint: Connected to barbarian royalty through marriage, he was installed and then died of disease within months.
description: Olybrius was a Roman nobleman living in Constantinople who was related to the Vandal royal family through marriage. He was installed as western emperor by the general Ricimer and died of natural causes after only about seven months.

name: Glycerius
hint: Replaced without a fight, he spent the rest of his life as a churchman.
description: Glycerius was a military official elevated to the western throne by the barbarian general Gundobad. He was deposed without bloodshed when the eastern court sent Julius Nepos as his replacement and was allowed to live out his days as a bishop in Dalmatia.

name: Julius Nepos
hint: Driven out of his own territory, he kept claiming the title from across the water until someone ended that too.
description: Julius Nepos was sent from Dalmatia with eastern backing to claim the western throne. He was driven out of Italy by his military commander Orestes after little more than a year and continued to claim the title from Dalmatia until his murder.

name: Romulus Augustulus
hint: A teenager whose deposition ended the empire with a whimper.
description: Romulus Augustulus was a teenager installed on the western throne by his father, the general Orestes. He was deposed by the Germanic chieftain Odoacer, who sent him into comfortable retirement in southern Italy, and he is traditionally considered the last western Roman emperor.

name: Leo I
hint: He spent years scheming to be free of the man who made him, then launched a naval disaster.
description: Leo I was a Thracian soldier elevated to the eastern throne by the Alan general Aspar, then spent much of his reign undermining Aspar's influence. He orchestrated Aspar's assassination and launched a disastrous naval expedition against the Vandals in North Africa.

name: Zeno
hint: A despised outsider from the mountains who got kicked out and came back.
description: Zeno was an Isaurian from the mountainous interior of Anatolia who was widely disliked as an outsider in Constantinople. He was briefly overthrown by Basiliscus but returned to power, and during his reign the western Roman Empire formally came to an end in 476 AD.

name: Basiliscus
hint: He grabbed power during a rival's absence and managed to offend everyone before starving in a cell.
description: Basiliscus was the brother-in-law of Leo I who seized the eastern throne after driving out Zeno. He alienated the church with unpopular religious policies and was overthrown when Zeno returned with Isaurian support, then was starved to death with his family.

name: Anastasius I
hint: An old man of different eye colours, who left the treasury overflowing.
description: Anastasius I was a former palace official chosen as eastern emperor at an advanced age, partly for his integrity. He reformed the empire's tax system and currency, left a massive surplus in the treasury, and was known for having two different colored eyes (heterochromia).

name: Justin I
hint: He couldn't read but had a nephew who could run an empire.
description: Justin I was an illiterate peasant from the Balkans who rose through the army to become commander of the palace guard, then emperor at about age sixty-eight. He relied heavily on his well-educated nephew Justinian for policy decisions.

name: Justinian I
hint: A law-codifying builder whose ambition was checked by plague.
description: Justinian I was the most ambitious ruler of the later Roman Empire, reconquering North Africa, Italy, and parts of Spain from barbarian kingdoms. He commissioned the Corpus Juris Civilis and the construction of the Hagia Sophia in Constantinople.

name: Justin II
hint: The strain of an overstretched inheritance broke his mind.
description: Justin II was Justinian I's nephew who inherited an overstretched empire and struggled to maintain its recent gains. He suffered a severe mental breakdown during his reign and had to hand effective control to the general Tiberius.

name: Tiberius II
hint: Known for giving away enormous sums of money, he got the job when his friend went crazy.
description: Tiberius II Constantine was a trusted general who governed for the mentally ill Justin II before formally taking power. Known for distributing enormous sums to the people, he chose his successor Maurice by giving his daughter in marriage.

name: Maurice
hint: A successful emperor, whose death at the hands of a centurion marked the start of the end.
description: Maurice was the son-in-law of Tiberius II who fought long wars against Persia and the Avars on the Danube frontier. His unpopular cost-cutting measures led to a military revolt under Phocas that cost Maurice his life and his entire family's lives.

name: Phocas
hint: A low-ranking officer who murdered his way to the top and was blamed by all for the troubles to come.
description: Phocas was a low-ranking army officer who led a mutiny that overthrew and murdered the emperor Maurice and his entire family. His brutal and incompetent reign saw the empire lose vast territory to Persia before he was overthrown and executed by Heraclius.

name: Heraclius
hint: Gambled and saved the empire, just to lose it all after.
description: Heraclius sailed from Africa to overthrow the tyrant Phocas, then lost nearly everything to a Persian invasion before launching a dramatic counter-campaign that destroyed the Sasanian Empire. He lived to see his reconquests immediately swept away by the Arab conquests.

name: Heraclius Constantine
hint: Decades as heir, months as ruler, then disease triggered a family crisis.
description: Heraclius Constantine (Constantine III) was the eldest son of Heraclius who had been co-emperor for many years. He held sole power for only a few months before dying of tuberculosis, triggering a succession crisis.

name: Heraclonas
hint: His mother's shadow and his predecessor's suspected poisoner hung over his brief reign.
description: Heraclonas was a teenage son of Heraclius who briefly held power alongside his young nephew. He was dominated by his mother Martina, who was suspected of poisoning his half-brother, and was deposed, had his nose cut off, and sent into exile.

name: Constans II
hint: He left the capital for a western island and was killed with a bathroom fixture.
description: Constans II took power as a young boy and ruled for over 26 years, controversially relocating from Constantinople to Sicily. He became the first emperor in centuries to visit Rome and was murdered in his bath in Syracuse, reportedly struck with a soap dish.

name: Constantine IV
hint: Gave arab ships a fiery end, bringing the first major Arab defeat in 50 years of expansion.
description: Constantine IV successfully defended Constantinople against a massive Arab naval siege lasting several years, employing the secret weapon known as Greek fire. He convened the Third Council of Constantinople (the sixth ecumenical council), which resolved the Monothelite controversy.

name: Justinian II
hint: A cut nose couldn't stop him from coming back.
description: Justinian II had his nose cut off when first overthrown, then spent years in exile among the Bulgars and Khazars. He married a Khazar princess, returned with a barbarian army to reclaim the throne, and ruled a second time before being overthrown again and killed.

name: Leontius
hint: Removed his predecessor's nose, only to have his own removed later.
description: Leontius was a general who overthrew Justinian II and seized the eastern throne. He launched a failed attempt to retake North Africa from the Arabs, was overthrown by the naval commander Apsimar (Tiberius III), had his nose cut off, and was later executed.

name: Tiberius III
hint: He changed his name to sound more imperial, but the man he'd deposed came back for revenge.
description: Tiberius III was originally a naval officer named Apsimar who took a more imperial name upon seizing power. He governed competently but was overthrown when the exiled Justinian II returned with a Bulgar army, and was publicly executed.

name: Philippicus
hint: He revived a condemned heresy and managed to anger both the pope and his own subjects.
description: Philippicus was of Armenian descent and took the throne after overthrowing the returned Justinian II. He promoted Monothelitism, a theological position condemned by the Sixth Ecumenical Council, and was deposed and blinded after less than two years.

name: Anastasius II
hint: His sensible military reforms were rewarded with a mutiny.
description: Anastasius II was a former bureaucrat who took power after a coup and attempted to reform the military and prepare defenses against the Arab threat. His plans provoked a mutiny, and he was overthrown and eventually executed.

name: Theodosius III
hint: A tax collector who never wanted the job and left it the moment someone better showed up.
description: Theodosius III was a reluctant tax collector forced into the purple by rebellious troops. He offered almost no resistance when the military commander Leo marched on Constantinople and abdicated to enter a monastery.

name: Leo III the Isaurian
hint: Ended the anarchy and defended the capital.
description: Leo III was a military commander from the eastern frontier who seized power and successfully repelled a major Arab siege of Constantinople in 717-718 AD. He initiated the policy of iconoclasm, the destruction of religious images, which divided the empire for over a century.

name: Constantine V
hint: His religious policy brought future condemnation, but his military success couldn't be erased.
description: Constantine V inherited the throne from Leo III but was briefly overthrown by his brother-in-law Artabasdos early in his reign. A capable military leader who won major victories against the Bulgars and Arabs, he was reviled by later church writers for his aggressive iconoclasm.

name: Artabasdos
hint: "Usurped the throne from the ""Dung-named"", just to lose it 2 years later."
description: Artabasdos was a son-in-law of Leo III who seized the throne from Constantine V, positioning himself as a defender of icons against iconoclasm. He was overthrown after about two years, blinded, and confined to a monastery.

name: Leo IV
hint: Named for his mother's steppe people, he may have died from putting on the wrong headgear.
description: Leo IV was known as 'the Khazar' after his mother's people and continued his father Constantine V's iconoclast policies in a somewhat milder form. He died young, possibly from a fever caused by wearing a sacred crown kept in the Hagia Sophia.

name: Constantine VI
hint: Blinded by his mum.
description: Constantine VI became emperor as a child under his mother Irene's regency. When he tried to assert his own authority as an adult, Irene overthrew him and had him blinded, likely fatally, making herself sole ruler.

name: Irene
hint: An ambitious woman whose gender brought a coronation in Rome.
description: Irene was an Athenian who married Emperor Leo IV and served as regent for her son Constantine VI. After blinding and deposing her own son, she became the first woman to rule the Roman Empire in her own right, which Charlemagne used to justify his own imperial coronation.

name: Nikephoros I
hint: A taxman whose skull became a barbarian king's drinking cup.
description: Nikephoros I was a former finance minister who overthrew the empress Irene and imposed unpopular taxes to rebuild the treasury. He was killed in a catastrophic defeat by the Bulgar Khan Krum, who allegedly had his skull lined with silver and used as a drinking cup.

name: Staurakios
hint: Paralyzed in the same attack that killed his father, he was pushed aside within weeks.
description: Staurakios was severely wounded in the same Bulgar ambush that killed his father Nikephoros I, leaving him paralyzed from the waist down. Unable to govern, he was pressured to abdicate after about two months in favor of his brother-in-law Michael I.

name: Michael I Rangabe
hint: He reversed his predecessors' religious policy but couldn't win a battle, so he became a monk.
description: Michael I Rangabe was a son-in-law of Nikephoros I who took the throne after his brother-in-law Staurakios's incapacitation. He reversed iconoclast policies but proved an ineffective military commander, losing the Battle of Versinikia to the Bulgars and abdicating to become a monk.

name: Leo V the Armenian
hint: Murdered on a holy morning in a chapel by friends of the man in his dungeon.
description: Leo V the Armenian was a general who took the throne after Michael I's military failures and revived the iconoclast policy. He was assassinated on Christmas Day in 820 AD in a chapel by supporters of Michael the Amorian, whom he had imprisoned.

name: Michael II the Amorian
hint: He was in chains waiting to die when his friends killed the ruler and set him free.
description: Michael II was a former soldier and childhood friend of Leo V who was in chains awaiting execution when his supporters assassinated Leo and freed him. He founded the Amorian dynasty and dealt with a major rebellion by Thomas the Slav.

name: Theophilos
hint: The last iconoclast, he loved eastern art and lost his hometown to the caliph.
description: Theophilos was the last Byzantine emperor to actively enforce iconoclasm and was a patron of the arts, fascinated by Arab culture. He suffered a major defeat when the Abbasid caliph al-Mu'tasim sacked his birthplace of Amorion in 838 AD.

name: Michael III
hint: Hostile sources call him a drunk, and he was killed by his stablehand.
description: Michael III became emperor as a small child under his mother Theodora's regency and presided over an important cultural and missionary revival. He was murdered by Basil the Macedonian, a former stable hand he had elevated to co-emperor.

name: Basil I the Macedonian
hint: From mucking out stalls to founding a dynasty, with a murder in between.
description: Basil I was an Armenian peasant who rose from stable hand to co-emperor by gaining Michael III's favor, then murdered his patron to seize the throne. Despite these brutal origins, he proved a capable ruler and founded the Macedonian dynasty.

name: Leo VI the Wise
hint: A wise man whose marriages brought scandal.
description: Leo VI 'the Wise' was a prolific legislator and literary figure whose four marriages at a time when the church barely tolerated a second caused a major scandal. His true parentage was widely debated, as he may have been the son of Michael III rather than Basil I.

name: Alexander
hint: A year of reckless behavior including insulting foreign envoys, possibly ended by a polo match.
description: Alexander ruled for barely a year after his brother Leo VI's long reign. He is mainly remembered for his reckless behavior, including provoking a war with the Bulgars by insulting their envoys, and died suddenly, possibly from exhaustion after a polo match.

name: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos
hint: Sidelined for decades by in-laws and co-rulers, he pursued scholarly pursuits instead.
description: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos was nominally emperor for over 46 years but was sidelined by his father-in-law Romanos I and other co-rulers for most of his reign. A prolific scholar, he produced detailed works including De Ceremoniis, De Administrando Imperio, and De Thematibus.

name: Romanos I Lekapenos
hint: Outmaneuvered his way into power, then his own sons betrayed him.
description: Romanos I Lekapenos was a naval commander who married his daughter to Constantine VII and gradually assumed power as senior co-emperor. He was overthrown by his own sons, who were themselves then ousted by Constantine VII.

name: Romanos II
hint: Dead at twenty-four, possibly poisoned, while his low-born wife went on to outlast everyone.
description: Romanos II married Theophano, a former tavern keeper's daughter who became one of the most powerful women in Byzantine history. He died at only about twenty-four, possibly poisoned, and Theophano went on to marry two successive emperors.

name: Nikephoros II Phokas
hint: An ascetic who slept on the floor and was murdered in his bed by his wife's lover.
description: Nikephoros II Phokas was a brilliant general who reconquered Crete and parts of Syria from the Arabs. So ascetic he slept on the floor, he was murdered in his bedchamber by John Tzimiskes with the help of Empress Theophano.

name: John I Tzimiskes
hint: He killed a ruler in bed with the empress's help, then conquered half the Middle East.
description: John I Tzimiskes was an Armenian general who murdered Nikephoros II Phokas in the imperial bedchamber with the help of Empress Theophano. He proved a formidable warrior, campaigning deep into Syria and the Balkans before dying suddenly, possibly poisoned.

name: Basil II the Bulgar-Slayer
hint: A man with a fitting nickname, whose lack of children brought turmoil.
description: Basil II came to power after a long struggle against powerful aristocratic families and became one of the most feared Byzantine emperors. He earned the nickname 'Bulgar-Slayer' for his brutal treatment of captured Bulgarian soldiers after the Battle of Kleidion in 1014 AD.

name: Constantine VIII
hint: He stood in a famous brother's shadow for decades and only ruled alone at the very end.
description: Constantine VIII was technically co-emperor alongside his brother Basil II for decades but only exercised sole power for the last three years of his life. He had no sons, and his two daughters Zoe and Theodora became crucial to the imperial succession.

name: Romanos III Argyros
hint: He thought he was a scholar and a soldier; he was neither, and was possibly poisoned by his wife.
description: Romanos III Argyros was a senior senator forced to marry the aging princess Zoe to become emperor. He fancied himself a great military leader and intellectual but failed at both, and was found dead, likely murdered by associates of Zoe.

name: Michael IV the Paphlagonian
hint: The empress fell for a commoner, her husband conveniently died, and the new man had seizures but governed well.
description: Michael IV the Paphlagonian was a handsome young man from a humble family who became emperor after catching Empress Zoe's eye. Despite suffering from severe epilepsy, he proved a surprisingly effective ruler and military commander before dying young.

name: Michael V Kalaphates
hint: He bit the hand that adopted him and the whole city rose up to punish him.
description: Michael V Kalaphates was adopted by Empress Zoe as her heir but then tried to exile her and rule independently. The people of Constantinople rose up in a massive riot in Zoe's defense, and Michael was dragged from a church, blinded, and deposed after only four months.

name: ZoÃƒÂ«
hint: Three husbands, a famous mosaic, and a lifetime locked in a palace until middle age.
description: Zoe Porphyrogenita was a princess kept secluded in the palace until middle age who became central to the Byzantine succession through three marriages to successive emperors. She briefly co-ruled with her sister Theodora and is depicted in a famous mosaic in the Hagia Sophia.

name: Theodora
hint: The quieter sister who outlasted everyone and refused to share power at the end.
description: Theodora Porphyrogenita was the sister of Empress Zoe, recalled from a convent to co-rule during a crisis. She later ruled alone as the last member of the Macedonian dynasty, refusing to marry and share power.

name: Constantine IX Monomachos
hint: His mistress lived openly in the palace and the churches split permanently on his watch.
description: Constantine IX Monomachos married the elderly Empress Zoe to claim the throne and proved an extravagant spender who depleted the treasury. He openly kept a mistress in the palace and presided over the Great Schism of 1054 between the Eastern and Western churches.

name: Michael VI Bringas
hint: A pencil-pusher who picked a fight with the generals and predictably lost.
description: Michael VI Bringas was an elderly bureaucrat appointed emperor by the dying Empress Theodora. He alienated the military aristocracy by favoring civilian officials and was overthrown within a year by a coalition of generals led by Isaac Komnenos.

name: Isaac I Komnenos
hint: He led a generals' revolt and tried to fix everything, but his health gave out first.
description: Isaac I Komnenos was a military aristocrat who led a revolt of eastern generals against Michael VI. He attempted sweeping reforms to restore the empire's finances and military strength but fell ill and abdicated after about two years to become a monk.

name: Constantine X Doukas
hint: He slashed the military budget just as mounted invaders appeared on the eastern horizon.
description: Constantine X Doukas was a leader of the civilian bureaucratic faction who took power after Isaac I's abdication. He neglected the army and reduced military spending at a time when the Seljuk Turks were emerging as a major threat on the eastern frontier.

name: Eudokia Makrembolitissa
hint: She broke a sacred oath not to remarry, and ended up in a convent for it.
description: Eudokia Makrembolitissa was the widow of Constantine X who served as regent for her young sons. She broke her oath not to remarry by marrying the general Romanos IV Diogenes but was forced into a convent when he was overthrown.

name: Romanos IV Diogenes
hint: He married a widow for the throne, rode east to fight, and came back blinded.
description: Romanos IV Diogenes was a military aristocrat who married the regent Eudokia to gain the throne and lead a campaign against the Seljuk Turks. He suffered a catastrophic defeat at the Battle of Manzikert in 1071 AD, was captured, and was later blinded and died from his injuries.

name: Michael VII Doukas
hint: A eunuch advisor manipulated the grain supply and the people gave him a mocking nickname.
description: Michael VII Doukas took the throne after Romanos IV's defeat at Manzikert and was dominated by the eunuch Nikephoritzes. He earned the mocking nickname 'Parapinakes' (minus a quarter) for debasing the coinage and was eventually forced to abdicate and become a bishop.

name: Nikephoros III Botaneiates
hint: Too old for the job from the start, he was quickly replaced by a younger military family.
description: Nikephoros III Botaneiates was an elderly general who overthrew Michael VII but could not control the empire's deteriorating military situation. He was quickly overthrown by the young general Alexios Komnenos and retired to a monastery.

name: Alexios I Komnenos
hint: Inherited a dumpster fire, spent 37 years putting it out, and actually succeeded.
description: Alexios I Komnenos seized power as a young general and spent his 37-year reign fighting to save the empire from collapse, dealing with Norman invasions, Seljuk advances, and the First Crusade. His life was recorded in detail by his daughter Anna Komnene in the Alexiad.

name: John II Komnenos
hint: Twenty-five years of virtuous campaigning ended by a suspicious hunting wound.
description: John II Komnenos is considered one of the most virtuous Byzantine emperors, spending his entire 25-year reign on campaign reconquering territory in Anatolia and northern Syria. He died from a wound caused by a poisoned arrow during a hunting accident, though some suspected foul play.

name: Manuel I Komnenos
hint: He jousted like a western knight but lost at a mountain pass.
description: Manuel I Komnenos was a charismatic emperor who admired Western European customs and pursued an ambitious foreign policy spanning from Egypt to Italy to Hungary. His defeat by the Seljuk Turks at the Battle of Myriokephalon in 1176 AD saw the final effort by the Byzantines to recover all of Anatolia.

name: Alexios II Komnenos
hint: A ten-year-old strangled with a bowstring by a power-hungry cousin.
description: Alexios II Komnenos came to the throne at about ten years old under his mother's regency. His cousin Andronikos I seized power, had the boy strangled with a bowstring, and married the child's betrothed, the young French princess Agnes of France.

name: Andronikos I Komnenos
hint: Decades as a fugitive and adventurer, then a brief tyranny ended by a mob.
description: Andronikos I Komnenos spent decades as a fugitive and adventurer across the eastern Mediterranean before seizing power in old age. He initially enacted popular reforms but became increasingly tyrannical and was torn apart by a mob in Constantinople.

name: Isaac II Angelos
hint: He killed his would-be arrester with his own hands, then his own brother took his eyes.
description: Isaac II Angelos came to power by personally killing the man sent to arrest him and rallying a mob in Constantinople. His reign saw Bulgarian independence reasserted and the Third Crusade pass through, before he was overthrown and blinded by his brother Alexios III.

name: Alexios III Angelos
hint: He blinded his brother for the throne, then ran without a fight when westerners came knocking.
description: Alexios III Angelos overthrew and blinded his own brother Isaac II to take the throne. His incompetent rule and refusal to honor obligations to the Fourth Crusade led directly to the crusaders' diversion against Constantinople, from which he fled without resistance.

name: Alexios IV Angelos
hint: He made promises to crusaders he couldn't keep, and a palace insider strangled him for it.
description: Alexios IV Angelos escaped captivity and convinced the leaders of the Fourth Crusade to help restore his blinded father Isaac II to the throne. When he could not fulfill his lavish promises of payment, the situation deteriorated and he was strangled by the palace official Alexios V.

name: Alexios V Doukas
hint: He strangled the last guy and tried to hold the walls, but the city fell for the first time to fellow Christians.
description: Alexios V Doukas (Mourtzouphlos) strangled Alexios IV and seized the throne during the crisis of the Fourth Crusade camped outside Constantinople. He tried to defend the city but failed, and Constantinople fell and was sacked by a Christian army for the first time in 1204 AD.

name: Theodore I Laskaris
hint: He fled to Anatolia after the capital fell and built a whole new state from scratch.
description: Theodore I Laskaris fled Constantinople after the Fourth Crusade's sack and established a government-in-exile in Nicaea. He built a new court and military, successfully defending his territory against both Latin occupiers and rival Greek successor states.

name: John III Doukas Vatatzes
hint: A farmer-emperor so beloved in exile that the local church made him a saint.
description: John III Doukas Vatatzes was the most successful emperor of the Nicaean exile, expanding territory in both Europe and Asia. He was so beloved that he was later venerated as a saint by the local church and nearly reconquered Constantinople before his death.

name: Theodore II Laskaris
hint: A sick intellectual who trusted commoners over nobles and paid for it with their hatred.
description: Theodore II Laskaris was a scholarly and intellectual emperor of Nicaea who suffered from severe epilepsy. He distrusted the aristocracy and promoted men of lower birth, making himself deeply unpopular with the nobility before dying young.

name: John IV Laskaris
hint: Blinded by his guardian on Christmas Day and locked away for decades.
description: John IV Laskaris was a child emperor of Nicaea who was blinded and deposed by his regent Michael Palaiologos on Christmas Day. He was confined to a fortress for the rest of his life, and his blinding led to the excommunication of Michael by the Patriarch.

name: Michael VIII Palaiologos
hint: He blinded a child, took back the great city, and tried to make peace with the western church.
description: Michael VIII Palaiologos was the regent who blinded the child emperor John IV Laskaris and then recaptured Constantinople from the Latin Empire in 1261 AD. He pursued a controversial union with the Western church to prevent further crusader attacks, making him deeply unpopular domestically.

name: Andronikos II Palaiologos
hint: Forty-five years of decline, a mercenary company gone rogue, and a grandson who overthrew him.
description: Andronikos II Palaiologos ruled for over 45 years but presided over a steady decline of the Byzantine Empire. He reversed his father's church union, hired the Catalan Company whose mercenaries ravaged his own territories, and was eventually deposed by his grandson Andronikos III.

name: Michael IX Palaiologos
hint: A lifetime as co-ruler without real power, ending in grief over a family accident.
description: Michael IX Palaiologos served as co-emperor alongside his father Andronikos II for decades but never held sole power. He was closely involved in the disastrous Catalan Company affair and reportedly died of grief after his son accidentally caused the death of his younger brother.

name: Andronikos III Palaiologos
hint: He accidentally killed his brother, overthrew his grandfather, then died young leaving chaos behind.
description: Andronikos III Palaiologos overthrew his grandfather Andronikos II in a civil war after accidentally causing his brother's death led to his disinheritance. He proved an active military ruler who personally led campaigns, but his early death left a young son and triggered another devastating civil war.

name: John V Palaiologos
hint: Fifty years on a shrinking throne, personally converting faiths for help that never came.
description: John V Palaiologos became emperor as a child and reigned for nearly 50 years as the empire shrank to almost nothing. He traveled to the west and personally converted to Catholicism seeking military aid against the Ottomans, but received little help and was repeatedly deposed by his own relatives.

name: John VI Kantakouzenos
hint: A regent who seized the throne, then gave it up to write history as a monk.
description: John VI Kantakouzenos was originally the chief minister and regent for the young John V before launching a civil war to seize power. He later abdicated and became a monk, writing a valuable history of his own times.

name: Andronikos IV Palaiologos
hint: He rebelled against his father, was partially blinded, and kept plotting anyway.
description: Andronikos IV Palaiologos rebelled against his father John V and briefly seized the throne with Ottoman and Genoese help. He was blinded as punishment, though not completely, and continued to plot, representing the tragic infighting of the dynasty's final century.

name: John VII Palaiologos
hint: A rebel who redeemed himself as a competent caretaker while his uncle begged for help abroad.
description: John VII Palaiologos briefly seized the throne from his grandfather John V with Ottoman support. He later reconciled with the family and served as a competent regent in Constantinople during a siege while his uncle Manuel II traveled in Western Europe.

name: Manuel II Palaiologos
hint: He toured the courts of western Europe hat in hand, and a Mongol warlord accidentally saved him.
description: Manuel II Palaiologos traveled extensively in Western Europe seeking military aid against the Ottomans, visiting the courts of France, England, and Aragon. He survived an Ottoman siege of Constantinople and benefited from Timur's defeat of Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara in 1402.

name: John VIII Palaiologos
hint: He agreed to church union in Italy, his people rejected it, and he watched the clock run out.
description: John VIII Palaiologos traveled to Italy to negotiate church union with the west at the Council of Florence, hoping to secure military help against the Ottomans. The union was agreed on paper but rejected by the Byzantine populace, and he spent his final years knowing the empire was doomed.

name: Constantine XI Palaiologos
hint: The last one standing, still fighting when the walls came down after a thousand years.
description: Constantine XI Palaiologos was the final Byzantine emperor, ruling a remnant state surrounded by Ottoman territory. He died defending Constantinople during the Ottoman siege of 1453, and his body was never conclusively identified.
